content.explanation:
The useEffect hook is React's primary mechanism for performing side effects in function components. Side effects are operations that reach outside the component's rendering logic — things like fetching data, subscribing to events, manually changing the DOM, or setting up timers. Before hooks, these operations were split across componentDidMount, componentDidUpdate, and componentWillUnmount in class components. useEffect unifies all three into a single API.

useEffect accepts two arguments: a setup function and an optional dependency array. The setup function runs after React commits changes to the DOM. If you return a function from the setup function, React treats it as a cleanup function and calls it before the effect runs again and when the component unmounts. This cleanup mechanism prevents memory leaks from subscriptions, timers, and other persistent resources.

The dependency array controls when the effect re-runs. If you pass an empty array [], the effect runs only once after the initial render — similar to componentDidMount. If you pass specific values [a, b], the effect re-runs only when those values change between renders. If you omit the array entirely, the effect runs after every render. Getting the dependency array right is one of the most important skills in React development.

React compares dependency values using Object.is (similar to ===). This means objects and arrays create new references on every render, which can cause effects to run more often than expected. Understanding reference equality is critical when working with useEffect dependencies.

In React 18's Strict Mode during development, effects are intentionally run twice (mount, unmount, mount) to help you find bugs related to missing cleanup functions. This does not happen in production builds.

codeExamples.title: Basic useEffect with dependency array
codeExamples.output:
Effect running for userId: 1
Rendered user: User 1
codeExamples.explanation: The effect runs after the component renders. The cleanup function runs before the next effect execution or when the component unmounts. The dependency array [userId] ensures the effect only re-runs when userId changes.

codeExamples.title: Empty dependency array vs no dependency array
codeExamples.output:
Mount effect: runs once
Render effect: count is 0
Count effect: count changed to 0
codeExamples.explanation: Three different dependency patterns: empty array for mount-only, no array for every render, and specific dependencies for selective re-runs.

codeExamples.title: Cleanup function for timers
codeExamples.explanation: The cleanup function clears the interval when the component unmounts, preventing memory leaks. Using the functional updater form (s => s + 1) avoids needing seconds in the dependency array.

notes: Effects run after the browser paints, not before. If you need to measure DOM layout before paint, use useLayoutEffect instead.
React's Strict Mode in development intentionally double-invokes effects to surface bugs with missing cleanup. This helps catch issues like duplicate subscriptions.
Always include all values from the component scope that change over time and are used inside the effect in the dependency array.
commonPitfalls: Forgetting to include dependencies in the array causes stale closures — the effect captures old values and never updates. The eslint-plugin-react-hooks 'exhaustive-deps' rule catches this.
Omitting the dependency array entirely causes the effect to run after every single render, which can lead to performance issues or infinite loops when the effect updates state.
interviewTips: Explain that useEffect replaces three class lifecycle methods: componentDidMount (empty deps), componentDidUpdate (with deps), and componentWillUnmount (cleanup return).
Mention that effects are asynchronous — they run after paint. This is different from useLayoutEffect which runs synchronously before paint.
Be ready to explain stale closures: when a dependency is missing, the effect closes over the initial value and never sees updates.

exercises.title: Document Title Effect
exercises.description: Create a useEffect that updates the document title whenever the count changes. Log the title being set.
exercises.expectedOutput:
Rendered with count: 0
Setting title to: Count is 0
hints: useEffect takes a function as its first argument and a dependency array as its second argument.
Include [count] as the dependency array so the effect re-runs when count changes.

exercises.title: Effect with Cleanup
exercises.description: Create a useEffect that logs subscribing on mount and unsubscribing on cleanup. The effect should only run once (on mount).
hints: Pass an empty dependency array [] to make the effect run only once on mount.
Return a function from the effect — React will call it when the component unmounts.

content.explanation:
useEffect becomes truly powerful when applied to real-world patterns like data fetching, event subscriptions, and timers. Each pattern has its own set of best practices and pitfalls. Understanding these patterns is essential for building robust React applications that handle asynchronous operations correctly.

Data fetching is the most common useEffect pattern. When fetching data based on props or state, you need to handle race conditions — situations where an older request completes after a newer one. The standard approach uses a boolean flag (often called 'ignore' or 'cancelled') that the cleanup function sets to true. When the fetch completes, it checks this flag before updating state. Modern applications can also use AbortController to actually cancel the HTTP request.

Event subscriptions follow a consistent pattern: subscribe in the effect body and unsubscribe in the cleanup function. This applies to DOM events (window resize, scroll), WebSocket connections, browser APIs (IntersectionObserver, ResizeObserver), and custom event emitters. The key rule is that every subscription must have a matching unsubscription in the cleanup.

Timers (setTimeout, setInterval) are another frequent pattern. setTimeout is straightforward — set it in the effect, clear it in cleanup. setInterval requires more care because the callback closes over state values. Using the functional updater form of setState (e.g., setCount(c => c + 1)) avoids stale closure issues with intervals.

A common advanced pattern is debouncing or throttling effects. For example, when implementing search-as-you-type, you want to delay the API call until the user stops typing. This combines setTimeout with cleanup: each keystroke clears the previous timeout and sets a new one.

codeExamples.title: Data fetching with race condition handling
codeExamples.output:
Render - loading: true data: null
Fetching data for user: 1
Data loaded: User 1
Render - loading: false data: User 1
codeExamples.explanation: The cancelled flag prevents state updates if the component unmounts or the userId changes before the fetch completes. This prevents the 'Can't perform a React state update on an unmounted component' warning.

codeExamples.title: Window event subscription
codeExamples.explanation: The effect subscribes to window resize events on mount and cleans up the listener on unmount. The empty dependency array ensures this runs only once.

codeExamples.title: Debounced search effect
codeExamples.output:
Results: 0
Setting debounce timer for: react
Searching for: react
Results: 1
codeExamples.explanation: Each time query changes, the previous timeout is cleared and a new one is set. Only the last query after 300ms of inactivity triggers the actual search.

notes: Always handle cleanup for subscriptions and timers. Leaked subscriptions cause memory leaks and can update state on unmounted components.
For data fetching, consider using libraries like React Query or SWR which handle caching, race conditions, and loading states automatically.
The functional updater form of setState (e.g., setCount(c => c + 1)) avoids the need to include the current state value in the dependency array.
commonPitfalls: Not handling race conditions in data fetching — if a user quickly switches between items, an older fetch might resolve after a newer one, showing stale data.
Using setInterval without the functional updater for setState causes stale closures. The callback captures the initial state value and never sees updates.

exercises.title: Fetch with Cancellation
exercises.description: Implement a useEffect that simulates fetching user data. Use a cancelled flag to prevent state updates after cleanup.
exercises.expectedOutput:
User: none
Fetching user: 42
User loaded: User 42
User: User 42
hints: Declare 'let cancelled = false' at the top of the effect. In the cleanup, set it to true.
Check 'if (!cancelled)' before calling setUser to prevent updates after unmount.

exercises.title: Timer with Cleanup
exercises.description: Create a useEffect that sets up a setTimeout to log a message after a delay. Clean up the timer properly.
exercises.expectedOutput:
Rendered with message: Hello
Timer set for: Hello
hints: Use setTimeout to schedule the delayed log and store the returned ID.
In the cleanup function, call clearTimeout with the stored ID to prevent the callback from firing after unmount.

content.explanation:
useRef returns a mutable ref object whose .current property is initialized to the passed argument. The returned object persists for the full lifetime of the component. Unlike state, mutating a ref does not cause a re-render. This makes useRef ideal for two distinct purposes: accessing DOM elements and storing mutable values that don't affect the visual output.

The most common use of useRef is to access DOM elements. By passing a ref object as the ref attribute on a JSX element, React sets ref.current to the corresponding DOM node after the component mounts. This lets you imperatively focus inputs, measure element dimensions, scroll to positions, or integrate with third-party DOM libraries. The ref is set to null when the element unmounts.

The second important use is storing mutable instance variables — values that persist across renders but whose changes don't need to trigger re-renders. Common examples include timer IDs, previous prop/state values, flags for tracking mount status, and counters for tracking renders. Think of useRef as a box that can hold any mutable value, similar to instance variables on a class.

A key distinction between useRef and useState is that updating ref.current does not cause a re-render. This is both a feature and a source of bugs. If you need the UI to reflect a value, use state. If you need to track a value without causing re-renders (like a timer ID or a cancelled flag), use a ref. Mixing these up is a common mistake.

Refs are also useful for capturing the previous value of a prop or state variable. By updating the ref in a useEffect, you can compare the current value with the previous one. This pattern is common enough that many codebases extract it into a usePrevious custom hook.

codeExamples.title: DOM ref for input focus
codeExamples.explanation: useRef(null) creates a ref that will hold a DOM element. The ref is attached via the ref attribute in JSX. In an effect, you can access the DOM node through ref.current.

codeExamples.title: Mutable ref for render counting
codeExamples.output:
Render count: 1
Type of renderCount: object
Has .current: true
codeExamples.explanation: Mutating renderCount.current does not trigger a re-render. The ref persists across renders, making it perfect for tracking values without causing update cycles.

codeExamples.title: useRef vs useState comparison
codeExamples.output:
refVal.current: 42
stateVal: 0
Ref update triggers re-render: no
State update triggers re-render: yes
codeExamples.explanation: This highlights the key difference: ref mutations are silent (no re-render), while state updates trigger re-renders. Choose based on whether the UI needs to reflect the value.

notes: useRef returns the same object on every render. It is a plain JavaScript object with a .current property — there is nothing special about it beyond React preserving it between renders.
Do not read or write ref.current during rendering (except for initialization). Reading refs during render makes your component's behavior unpredictable.

For callback refs (functions passed as the ref attribute), React calls the function with the DOM node on mount and null on unmount. This is useful when you need to know exactly when a ref is attached.
commonPitfalls: Using useRef when useState is needed — if the value change should be reflected in the UI, use state. Ref mutations won't trigger a re-render to show the new value.
Accessing ref.current in the render body for DOM refs before the ref is attached. DOM refs are null during the first render and only populated after React updates the DOM.

exercises.title: Persistent Counter with Ref
exercises.description: Use useRef to track how many times a function component has been called (rendered) without causing additional re-renders.
exercises.expectedOutput:
Render #1
Ref is persistent: true
Render #2
Ref is persistent: true
hints: Use React.useRef(0) to create a ref initialized to 0.
Increment ref.current directly — this does not cause a re-render.

exercises.title: Previous Value Tracker
exercises.description: Use useRef combined with useEffect to track the previous value of a prop and log both current and previous values.
hints: Initialize the ref with useRef(undefined) so the first render shows no previous value.
Update the ref inside useEffect so it always lags one render behind the current value.

content.explanation:
The useContext hook provides a way to pass data through the component tree without manually threading props through every level. It solves the problem known as 'prop drilling' — where intermediate components receive and forward props they don't actually use, just so deeply nested components can access them. Context is ideal for global or semi-global data like themes, authentication state, locale preferences, and UI settings.

To use context, you first create one with React.createContext(defaultValue). This returns an object with two components: Provider and Consumer (though the Consumer is rarely used now that useContext exists). The Provider wraps a portion of the component tree and accepts a value prop. Any component inside that Provider subtree can read the context value using useContext(MyContext).

The defaultValue passed to createContext is only used when a component calls useContext but there is no matching Provider above it in the tree. This is useful for testing or for standalone component usage. In most applications, the Provider always exists, so the default value acts as a fallback or type hint.

When the Provider's value changes, all components consuming that context re-render. This is an important performance consideration. If the value is an object created inline in the Provider's render (e.g., value={{ user, theme }}), a new object is created every render, causing all consumers to re-render even if the data hasn't changed. The solution is to memoize the value with useMemo.

Context is not a replacement for all state management. It works best for data that changes infrequently (theme, auth, locale). For frequently changing data or complex state logic, dedicated state management libraries or useReducer combined with context may be more appropriate. Context + useReducer is often called 'poor man's Redux' and is sufficient for many applications.

codeExamples.title: Creating and consuming context
codeExamples.explanation: Without a Provider, useContext returns the defaultValue passed to createContext. When wrapped in a Provider, it returns the Provider's value prop instead.

codeExamples.title: Context with state management
codeExamples.output:
UserProvider created with: Alice
ProfileHeader - name: Alice
ProfileHeader - role: admin
codeExamples.explanation: In a real app, the Provider would wrap the tree and useContext would read the value. Here we simulate the data flow to show how context values are consumed by deeply nested components.

codeExamples.title: Multiple contexts
codeExamples.output:
Theme: light
Language: en
Contexts are independent: true
codeExamples.explanation: Components can consume multiple contexts. Each useContext call subscribes to one specific context. Contexts are independent — updating one does not affect consumers of another.

notes: useContext always reads the value from the nearest Provider above the calling component. If no Provider is found, it uses the defaultValue from createContext.
Context consumers re-render whenever the Provider's value changes. Use useMemo on the value object to prevent unnecessary re-renders.
Context is best for low-frequency updates (theme, auth, locale). For high-frequency updates (mouse position, animations), consider other approaches to avoid excessive re-renders.
commonPitfalls: Creating a new object for the Provider value on every render (e.g., value={{ user, setUser }}) causes all consumers to re-render. Wrap the value in useMemo to maintain referential equality.
Using context for every piece of shared state. Context re-renders all consumers when the value changes, so putting fast-changing values (like input text) in context can cause performance problems.

exercises.title: Theme Context
exercises.description: Create a ThemeContext with a default value and a component that reads from it using useContext.
hints: React.createContext('light') creates a context with 'light' as the default value.
React.useContext(ThemeContext) reads the current value from the nearest ThemeContext.Provider, or the default if no Provider exists.

exercises.title: Auth Context Pattern
exercises.description: Simulate an auth context pattern by creating a context, a function that provides user data, and a consumer component.
exercises.expectedOutput:
Auth provider initialized
Logged in: true
User: Alice
hints: The default value for createContext should match the shape of the data you plan to provide.
In a real app, useContext(AuthContext) replaces the manual parameter passing shown here.

content.explanation:
React hooks follow two fundamental rules that must never be broken: (1) Only call hooks at the top level — never inside loops, conditions, or nested functions; and (2) Only call hooks from React function components or custom hooks — never from regular JavaScript functions. These rules exist because of how React internally tracks hook state using a linked list tied to call order.

The first rule — calling hooks at the top level — is the most critical. React does not identify hooks by name. Instead, it relies on the order in which hooks are called during each render. On the first render, React creates a linked list of hook states. On subsequent renders, React walks through this list in the same order, matching each hook call to its stored state. If you put a hook inside a conditional, the order of hook calls might change between renders, causing React to match the wrong state to the wrong hook.

Consider what happens when a useState hook is inside an if statement. On the first render, the condition might be true, so React records: [useState, useEffect]. On the next render, the condition might be false, skipping the useState. React now sees: [useEffect], but it tries to match the first call to the first stored state (which was useState's state). This mismatch corrupts the entire hook state for that component, leading to unpredictable bugs.

The second rule — only calling hooks from React functions — ensures that hooks have access to the component's fiber node (React's internal representation). Regular functions don't have a fiber context, so hooks can't store or retrieve their state. Custom hooks work because they are ultimately called from within a component's render, which has the fiber context.

The eslint-plugin-react-hooks package provides two rules: 'rules-of-hooks' (enforces the two rules above) and 'exhaustive-deps' (warns about missing dependencies in useEffect, useMemo, and useCallback). These rules are essential for catching hook-related bugs at development time. The rules-of-hooks ESLint rule is considered so important that it should always be set to 'error', not 'warn'.

codeExamples.title: Correct vs incorrect hook usage
codeExamples.explanation: Always call all hooks at the top of your component, then use conditional logic afterward. Never skip hook calls based on conditions.

codeExamples.title: Hook call order demonstration
codeExamples.output:
Hook slot 0: first
Hook slot 1: second
Hook slot 2 (ref): 1
Total hooks called: 3
codeExamples.explanation: React tracks hooks by their call order (0, 1, 2...). The same hooks must be called in the same order on every render for React to correctly match each call to its stored state.

codeExamples.title: Valid hook contexts
codeExamples.output:
useCounter hook called, count: 10
MyComponent rendered, count: 10
codeExamples.explanation: Hooks can be called from function components and custom hooks (functions starting with 'use'). They cannot be called from regular functions, class components, or outside of React's render cycle.

notes: The eslint-plugin-react-hooks package should be installed in every React project. The 'rules-of-hooks' rule should be set to 'error' to catch violations at compile time.
Custom hooks must start with 'use' (e.g., useAuth, useFetch). This naming convention allows the ESLint plugin to verify that hooks are called correctly inside them.
If you need conditional behavior, call the hook unconditionally and use the condition inside the hook's logic or after the hook call.
commonPitfalls: Calling hooks inside loops or conditions. If you need conditional effects, call useEffect unconditionally and put the condition inside the effect body: useEffect(() => { if (condition) { ... } }, [condition]).
Calling hooks from event handlers or callbacks. Hooks must be called during the component's render phase, not in response to events. Event handlers should call state setters, not hooks.
interviewTips: Explain that React uses a linked list indexed by call order, not by hook name. This is why call order must be stable across renders.
Mention the two rules explicitly: (1) only at the top level, (2) only from React functions or custom hooks. Then explain the WHY — React's internal linked list implementation.
Be ready to give an example of what goes wrong when hooks are called conditionally — state values get mismatched with the wrong hooks, leading to corrupted component state.

exercises.title: Fix Conditional Hook
exercises.description: Refactor a component that incorrectly uses a hook inside a condition. Move the hook to the top level and use the condition after the hook call.
hints: Move the useState call to the top of the function, before any conditions.
Use the isLoggedIn condition after the hook to decide what to log — the hook must always be called.

exercises.title: Hook Order Verification
exercises.description: Create a component that demonstrates consistent hook ordering by calling multiple hooks and logging their slot positions.
exercises.expectedOutput:
Slot 0 (state): React
Slot 1 (state): 18
Slot 2 (ref): stable
Hook order is consistent: true
hints: Call useState twice and useRef once, all at the top level before any logic.
The slot numbers correspond to the order in which hooks are called — first hook is slot 0, second is slot 1, etc.
